title: Consciousness
whatItIs:
Consciousness is the phenomenon of subjective experience — the fact that there is something it is like to be you. When you see the colour red, feel pain, taste coffee, or experience the emotion of nostalgia, there is a qualitative, first-person character to that experience that seems fundamentally different from any objective description of the underlying neural activity. This gap between the objective processes in the brain and the subjective experience they give rise to is what philosopher David Chalmers called the hard problem of consciousness, and it remains one of the deepest unsolved problems in both science and philosophy.

The study of consciousness sits at the intersection of neuroscience, philosophy of mind, psychology, and increasingly, computer science and artificial intelligence. Neuroscientists search for the neural correlates of consciousness — the specific patterns of brain activity that correspond to conscious experience. Philosophers analyse the conceptual foundations: what exactly we mean by consciousness, whether it can be reduced to physical processes, and whether it is even possible in principle to explain subjective experience in objective terms. Psychologists study how conscious awareness relates to attention, perception, memory, and decision-making. And AI researchers are confronting the question of whether the systems they build could ever be conscious, and what moral obligations would follow if they were.

Several major theories compete to explain consciousness. Integrated Information Theory (IIT), developed by Giulio Tononi, proposes that consciousness corresponds to a system's capacity to integrate information in a way that is both differentiated and unified, and assigns a numerical value (phi) to a system's level of consciousness. Global Workspace Theory (GWT), originated by Bernard Baars, models consciousness as a broadcasting mechanism in the brain — information becomes conscious when it is made available to a wide network of specialised processors through a central workspace. Higher-Order Theories propose that a mental state becomes conscious when the brain generates a higher-order representation of that state — essentially, a thought about a thought. Each theory captures something important, but none has achieved consensus, and it is not yet clear whether consciousness can be explained by any single framework.
whyItMatters:
Understanding consciousness matters because it touches the foundation of what it means to be a person, and it has direct practical consequences for medicine, ethics, law, and technology. In clinical medicine, the question of whether a patient in a vegetative state or under anaesthesia retains any form of conscious experience is not academic — it determines how they are treated, whether life support is continued, and what counts as an ethically acceptable level of care. Recent neuroscience research using fMRI has shown that some patients diagnosed as being in a persistent vegetative state can respond to commands by modulating their brain activity, suggesting islands of consciousness that were invisible to behavioural assessment.

The question of animal consciousness has profound ethical implications for how we treat other species. If a pig or an octopus has genuine subjective experience — if there is something it is like to be them — then industrial farming practices that cause suffering take on a different moral weight than if these animals are merely biological automatons. The philosophical and scientific assessment of animal consciousness directly shapes animal welfare legislation, farming regulations, and the ethics of scientific experimentation on animals.

As artificial intelligence systems become more sophisticated, the question of machine consciousness has moved from science fiction to active research. If a future AI system were genuinely conscious, it would arguably have moral status — interests that deserve consideration, perhaps even rights. If it were not conscious but merely behaving as if it were, different moral conclusions would follow. The inability to resolve this question with current tools means that AI ethics is building on philosophical foundations that are not yet solid, making the study of consciousness directly relevant to some of the most consequential technology decisions of the coming decades.
keyIdeas: The hard problem of consciousness — why physical processes give rise to subjective experience
Qualia — the subjective, qualitative character of conscious experiences
Neural correlates of consciousness — brain activity patterns associated with awareness
Integrated Information Theory (IIT) — consciousness as integrated information
Global Workspace Theory — consciousness as a broadcasting mechanism in the brain
Higher-Order Theories — consciousness as the brain's representation of its own states
The explanatory gap — the difficulty of bridging objective description and subjective experience
Panpsychism — the view that consciousness is a fundamental feature of all matter
The binding problem — how the brain unifies separate sensory inputs into a single experience
Altered states — what dreams, psychedelics, meditation, and anaesthesia reveal about awareness
frontier:
The frontier of consciousness research is being reshaped by advances in neuroscience, psychedelic science, and artificial intelligence. Neuroscience is making progress in identifying the neural signatures of consciousness using increasingly precise tools — high-density EEG, intracranial recordings, and real-time fMRI — to track when and where conscious awareness emerges in the brain. The Perturbational Complexity Index (PCI), developed by Marcello Massimini, uses targeted brain stimulation to measure the complexity of the brain's response, and has shown remarkable accuracy in distinguishing conscious from unconscious states in patients under anaesthesia or in disorders of consciousness. This is moving the field from philosophical speculation toward empirical testability.

Psychedelic research has experienced a dramatic revival, with clinical trials of psilocybin, DMT, and MDMA generating new data about altered states of consciousness. These substances produce profound changes in subjective experience — dissolution of the sense of self, synesthetic merging of senses, experiences of cosmic unity — while generating distinctive neural signatures that can be measured and analysed. This research is providing something the field has long lacked: a controlled experimental lever for modulating consciousness, allowing researchers to study what changes in the brain when the character of experience changes dramatically.

The AI consciousness debate has intensified as large language models demonstrate increasingly sophisticated behaviour. Whether these systems have any form of subjective experience is an open question that current science cannot definitively answer, because we lack an agreed-upon test for consciousness that does not rely on self-report or behaviour alone. Some researchers, drawing on IIT, argue that current digital architectures lack the integrated information structure needed for consciousness. Others argue that we simply do not know enough to rule it out. Contemplative science — the systematic study of meditation and introspective practices from Buddhist, Hindu, and secular traditions — offers a complementary approach, using trained first-person observation to map the structure of conscious experience from the inside. The integration of neuroscience, psychedelic research, AI, and contemplative practice represents the most promising multidisciplinary approach to consciousness that has ever existed.
openQuestions: Why does subjective experience exist at all — why is there something it is like to be a conscious organism?
Can consciousness be fully explained by physical processes, or does it require new fundamental principles?
Is consciousness binary (present or absent) or does it exist on a continuum of degrees?
Could an artificial system be genuinely conscious, and how would we detect it if it were?
What is the relationship between consciousness and attention — can you be conscious of something you are not attending to?
Does consciousness play a causal role in behaviour, or is it an epiphenomenon — a byproduct of neural activity with no functional effect?
Is panpsychism a viable scientific hypothesis, or a philosophical dead end?
What do psychedelic experiences reveal about the default structure of ordinary waking consciousness?
